title: Factfulness: Ten Reasons We're Wrong About the World — and Why Things Are Better Than You Think
excerpt: Hans Rosling's revolutionary book about why our instincts lead us to misunderstand the world — and how a fact-based worldview reveals that global progress is far greater than most people realize.
keyIdeas: Most people systematically misunderstand the state of the world — believing things are worse than they are
Our dramatic instincts evolved to help us survive but lead us to systematically underestimate progress
A fact-based worldview is not optimistic or pessimistic — it is accurate
keyTakeaways: Global extreme poverty has fallen from 85% to 9% in 200 years
80% of the world's children are now vaccinated
Girls' education and child mortality have improved dramatically
Most people live in middle-income countries, not rich or poor
The world is better than it has ever been, but we fail to see it
whoShouldRead: Anyone who feels anxious about the state of the world
News consumers who want to understand media bias toward negativity
Students of public health, development, and global affairs
Anyone interested in critical thinking and overcoming cognitive biases
whoShouldSkip: Those who are deeply pessimistic and prefer to remain so
Readers seeking detailed policy proposals rather than a framework
People who believe that acknowledging progress is naive
